hero.subtitle: 35,000+ Australians standing against the $11.4B VNI West transmission line — and the corporate thugs sent to bully farmers off their own land.

intro.heading: Farmer-led. Community-funded. Non-partisan.
intro.lede: Farmers Fightback is led by the Wallaloo & Gre Gre District Association — a farmer-led coalition from the heart of Australia's broadacre farming belt. We are not activists. We are not funded by corporations or political parties. We are farming families who were told that multinational energy companies have more right to our land than we do. And we said no.

presidentBlock.name: Ben Duxson
presidentBlock.title: President, Wallaloo & Gre Gre District Association
bioParagraphs: Our president, Ben Duxson, is a sixth-generation Kanya farmer. His family has been on this land for over a century. He didn't choose this fight. None of us did. But when corporate contractors started arriving on soldier settlement farms — farms built by diggers who fought for this country — reading from scripts and threatening fines, we didn't have a choice.

This is a genuine, non-partisan, community-funded movement. Every dollar comes from individual Australians. Every volunteer comes from farming communities and the suburbs. Every supporter shares the same belief: that if the government can do this to a farmer, they can do it to anyone.
presidentBlock.tagline: We're not anti-renewables. We're anti being walked over.

supporters.title: Farming families
supporters.body: Across Australia, who understand what it means to have your livelihood threatened by a government decision made hundreds of kilometres away.

supporters.title: Returned servicemen & women
supporters.body: And their families — people for whom the soldier settlement connection is personal and the ANZAC Day timing is an affront.

supporters.title: Property rights advocates
supporters.body: Australians who defend castle doctrine and believe that your gate is your gate, regardless of who is standing on the other side of it.

supporters.title: Rural community members
supporters.body: Small business owners, tradespeople, teachers, and healthcare workers who know that when farms die, towns die.

supporters.title: Urban supporters
supporters.body: In Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, and beyond who see the broader principle: if government contractors can enter your property without identifying themselves, nobody's home is safe.

supporters.title: Food security advocates
supporters.body: Australians concerned about food security and the future of Australian agriculture.

media.heading: Coverage of the Farmers Fightback story.
media.lede: Despite national significance, the major metro broadcasters have largely ignored this story. We invite every media outlet in Australia to watch the footage on this page and then explain to their audience why this story doesn't deserve coverage.

evidence.heading: The evidence they don't want you to see.
evidence.intro: Raw footage. Real farmers. Real threats. This is what's happening to Australian farming families — and nobody's talking about it.
paragraphsHtml: Across Australia, farming families are being targeted by energy corporations backed by government power. Corporate contractors arrive unannounced on private land — land that has been in families for generations, some of it soldier settlement country granted to returned servicemen after the World Wars. They read from legal scripts. They threaten fines of up to $6,000. They demand access to property without proper notice, without consent, and without a shred of respect.

They dress like authority. They talk like authority. They are not authority. They are employees of multinational energy corporations — companies worth tens of billions of dollars — sent to bully ordinary Australians off their own land so transmission towers can be bulldozed through the nation's food bowl.

Every piece of evidence on this page is real. Filmed by the farmers themselves. Exposed by a movement that started with a handful of families talking around a kitchen table and has grown into a national campaign with 35,000+ supporters, 36,000+ Facebook followers, and hundreds of donors from every state and territory.
evidence.callout: The government won't investigate. The media won't cover it. The corporations won't stop. So we're putting the evidence where everyone can see it.

originParagraphs: Farmers Fightback began in the paddocks and kitchens of Western Victoria. Not in a boardroom. Not in a political office. On the land.

The Wallaloo and Gre Gre districts sit in the heart of Australia's broadacre farming belt. These are soldier settlement properties — land granted to returned servicemen after the First and Second World Wars so they could build a life and feed a nation. Families have been farming here for generations. Some properties have been in the same family for over a century.

When the government announced an $11.4 billion transmission line that would cut directly through this country — through the farms of digger families, through land that has been farmed, cared for, and defended for generations — local farmers formed the Wallaloo & Gre Gre District Association to fight back.
What started as a few families became a district. Then a region. Then a national movement.

numbers.label: Failure rate
numbers.sub: VicGrid hasn't accessed a property in 3 weeks

movement.campaignsLede: Farmers Fightback runs three interconnected campaigns. Each addresses a different dimension of the crisis facing rural Australia. Together, they build a picture of systemic government failure.

campaigns.title: VNI West Launch Campaign
campaigns.body: The original fight. Corporate contractors arriving unannounced on properties. Farmers who asked for identification threatened with fines. Property rights decimated. Farms critical to the Australian food bowl treated like a gap on a map.

campaigns.title: Fuel & Fertiliser Crisis
campaigns.body: While the VNI West fight made headlines, another crisis was crushing farming families. Fuel and fertiliser costs rose 200% in a single year. Crop prices stalled. The maths stopped working. Input costs exceeded returns. Farmers were being asked to plant at a loss.

campaigns.title: Hold the Gate
campaigns.body: The centrepiece action. A farm gate compliance operation across soldier settlement properties. Volunteers from across Australia standing with farming families at their gates. Not to confront. To document. To observe. Every contractor is asked to identify themselves. Every interaction is filmed and logged.

evidence.shortsLede: Short, sharp, undeniable. Each clip is under a minute. Each one shows a corporate contractor threatening an Australian farmer on their own land. Watch them all. Then ask yourself why this isn't on the nightly news.

shorts.title: "Anything you do or say may be used as evidence."
shorts.quote: Anything you do or say may be used as evidence against you.
shorts.body: A corporate contractor arrives unannounced at a family farm gate and reads from a scripted legal warning — word for word — as if they were police. They're not police. They have no authority. But they've been trained to speak like they do. Watch as the farmer stands their ground while a hired suit tries to intimidate them off their own property.
shorts.proves: Energy companies are coaching private contractors to use police-style language to intimidate farmers. This is deliberate psychological pressure designed to make landholders believe they have no rights. They do.

shorts.title: Corporate thugs pretending to be cops.
shorts.quote: They dress like authority. They talk like authority. They are not authority.
shorts.body: Contractors sent by energy companies arrive at farm gates wearing high-vis and lanyards, carrying clipboards and reading from scripts designed to sound like official law enforcement. They demand access. They cite legislation. They threaten fines. But they are private employees of private corporations — with no more legal authority than a door-to-door salesman.
shorts.proves: This is a coordinated strategy. Energy corporations are deliberately dressing and scripting contractors to impersonate authority figures. Australian farmers are being psychologically manipulated on their own land by people who have zero legal power over them.

shorts.title: Silence is now a crime on your own farm.
shorts.quote: You're standing on your own property. Saying nothing. And they're telling you that's a crime.
shorts.body: A farmer stands silently at their own gate. They don't yell. They don't threaten. They don't obstruct. They simply exist — on their own land — and refuse to engage with uninvited corporate contractors. The contractor's response? To warn them that their silence will be used against them. That doing nothing, on your own property, is now apparently an offence.
shorts.proves: When silence on your own land is treated as obstruction, the law has been turned upside down. This footage shows exactly how far energy companies will go to strip farmers of their most basic right — the right to say nothing and be left alone on their own property.

shorts.title: No social license. No respect. No idea.
shorts.quote: No social license. No respect. No idea what they're walking into.
shorts.body: Corporate contractors arrive at a farm with zero community consultation, zero local knowledge, and zero respect for the people who work the land. They have clipboards and scripts but no understanding of the community they are about to destroy. This is what happens when billion-dollar energy projects are imposed from capital cities onto people who were never asked.
shorts.proves: The complete absence of genuine consultation. These projects have no social license. The communities affected were never meaningfully consulted. The contractors sent to enforce access don't even know what the farms produce or who lives there. This is corporate colonialism in broad daylight.

shorts.title: They gave us 30 minutes notice. The law says 48 hours.
shorts.quote: The law says 48 hours. They gave us 30 minutes. Because the law doesn't apply when the government is the one breaking it.
shorts.body: Contractors give a farming family just 30 minutes' notice before attempting to access their property. The law requires 48 hours. Over 100 community members dropped everything and stood at the gate. The contractors didn't show. 100% failure rate across three weeks. Not one property accessed. Not a single boot on a single paddock.
shorts.proves: The government can't even follow its own laws. The same authorities that threaten farmers with thousands of dollars in fines for standing on their own property can't comply with basic notice requirements that they wrote themselves. Rules for us, not for them.

evidence.longsLede: The shorts give you 60 seconds. These give you the full picture. Every word. Every threat. Every moment a corporate contractor tried to bully an Australian farmer — and failed.

longs.title: Farmer schooled the corporate thug he warned not to come.
longs.quote: I warned you not to come. You came anyway. Now the whole country is watching.
longs.body: A farmer confronts a corporate contractor who was explicitly warned not to return to the property. The contractor came anyway — with a clipboard, a script, and the backing of a multinational energy corporation worth tens of billions. What follows is five minutes of a farmer calmly, clearly, and devastatingly dismantling every claim the contractor makes. No threats. No violence. Just truth — and a camera rolling.
longs.proves: Australian farmers know their rights better than the corporations trying to take their land. This footage went viral because it shows what happens when an ordinary person refuses to be bullied by corporate power. 92,000 views and counting. The contractors are trained. The farmers are ready.

longs.title: Two farmers threatened in two days.
longs.quote: Two farms. Two days. Same script. Same threats. Same corporation.
longs.body: In the space of 48 hours, two separate farming families on two separate properties were visited by corporate contractors using identical scripts, identical threats, and identical intimidation tactics. Same company. Same playbook. Different families who didn't know each other — until now. This isn't a one-off. This is a systematic, industrialised campaign of intimidation targeting Australian farming families.
longs.proves: This is not isolated. This is coordinated corporate intimidation on a national scale. When two families in different locations receive identical threats within 48 hours, you're not looking at a rogue contractor — you're looking at a corporate program designed to break farmers one family at a time.

ctaParagraphsHtml: Every video on this page was filmed by an Australian farmer on their own property. Every statistic is real. Every claim can be verified. This is not a fringe issue. This is 35,000 Australians — from every state and territory — who have drawn a line.
The government won't investigate. The media won't cover it. The corporations won't stop.
So we're asking you to do three things:
ctaSteps: Watch the footage. All of it. Then make up your own mind.
Share it. Send it to everyone you know. Post it. Email it. Text it. Make it impossible to ignore.
Sign the petition. Add your name to the 35,000+ Australians who have already drawn the line.
evidence.ctaCloser: Because if this can happen to a farmer, it can happen to anyone.
evidence.shareText: 35,000 Australians have drawn the line. Corporate contractors are threatening farmers on soldier settlement land — land built by diggers. We filmed everything. Watch the evidence. #FarmersFightback #HandsOffOurLand #HoldTheGate

summary.heading: The $11.4 billion line they're bulldozing through our food bowl.
paragraphsHtml: VNI West is a 400-kilometre high-voltage transmission line proposed to cut through some of Australia's most productive cropping and grazing country. Originally costed at $3.8 billion, it blew out to $7.6 billion. Current estimates put it at $11.4 billion — the most expensive transmission project in Australian history.
Behind it sits AEMO, AusNet, and Iberdrola — an €80-billion Spanish multinational now sending contractors onto family farms, unannounced, with threats and clipboards.

The proposed route cuts directly through soldier settlement farms — land granted to returned servicemen after the World Wars. Families were never meaningfully consulted. Alternative routes were never seriously considered. The farms were treated like a gap on a map.
If contractors can enter your property without identifying themselves, that precedent doesn't stop at one farm gate. It applies to every Australian property.

petition.heading: Add your name. Draw the line.
petition.lede: Every signature is a letter to Spring Street and Canberra. It tells them Victorian farmers aren't a speed bump — and it tells the next farmer facing a contractor at the gate that they're not alone.
bullets: Your details stay on-shore and are never sold
Weekly campaign updates — unsubscribe any time
Authorised by Ben Duxson, Wallaloo VIC

quote.html: They came back two days later. Mid-lambing. Reading from a script. Telling me I'd cop a five-hundred-thousand-dollar fine if I didn't let them through my own front gate.
quote.name: — Rob, fourth-generation farmer

donate.heading: Every dollar fights for a farm.
donate.body: Legal advice for farmers on the corridor. Camera gear for the next confrontation. Printing, fuel, hall hire, ads that won't air on commercial radio. We run lean. You keep us in the paddock.

tiers.label: Fills a ute for a week

donate.fineprint: Secure processing via Stripe. All amounts in AUD. Receipts sent to your inbox. Not tax-deductible.

newsletter.heading: Get the next video before the nightly news does.
newsletter.lede: Weekly dispatches from the corridor. No spam, no corporate chaff.
newsletter.doneText: ✓ You're on the list. Keep an eye on your inbox.
